english: Beef Carpaccio with Rocket and Parmesan Pizza

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef carpaccio pizza; FOODON_00002572 (pizza food product) used as the closest parent class. Carpaccio topping variant is not individually coded in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pizza — composite dish with meat topping
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pizza as a composite dish; no specific code for carpaccio-topped pizza exists. Used as closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef carpaccio pizza as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: dressed with aged balsamic and extra virgin olive oil

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a whole 12-inch pizza weighing approximately 400 g, typically shared between two people or consumed as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, raw, composite cuts; Pizza, cheese; Arugula, raw; Tomatoes, cherry; Parmesan cheese; Mozzarella cheese; Balsamic vinegar; Olive oil
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for beef carpaccio pizza as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for raw beef (FDC ID 174032), cheese pizza base (FDC ID 170720), arugula (FDC ID 169387), cherry tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), Parmesan (FDC ID 173420), mozzarella (FDC ID 173420), balsamic vinegar (FDC ID 173469), and extra virgin olive oil (FDC ID 171413). The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these USDA component references and are broadly consistent with a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate pizza variant. Input values retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reliably disaggregated for this composite; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Pizza, cheese (thin crust) GI ~45 used as analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for beef carpaccio pizza. The closest approved analogous entry is thin-crust cheese pizza (GI ~45, low category) from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI Values. This dish has substantially lower carbohydrate content (3.33 g/100g) than a standard cheese pizza due to the dominance of raw beef and cheese toppings over dough; the GI of 45 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.32) / 100 = 5.99 ≈ 6.0, where carbohydrate per 400g serving = 3.33 × 4 = 13.32 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100g; Parmesan cheese and added salt are primary contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium (52% of the 2300 mg daily limit); request reduced Parmesan and minimal added salt, and note that the dominant fat from extra virgin olive oil is cardioprotective unsaturated fat which partially offsets cardiovascular sodium risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.33 g/100g) and low sugar (2.0 g/100g); high protein (16.67 g/100g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycaemic load (~6 per full pizza serving) due to minimal dough and high protein from raw beef and cheese, making it a suitable occasional choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, if a thick dough base is used, carbohydrate content increases substantially and the dish should be requested without or with minimal dough.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.67 g/100g from beef and cheese; trans fat 0.33 g/100g; cholesterol 46.67 mg/100g; partially offset by extra virgin olive oil (monounsaturated fat)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat and trans fat content from raw beef and aged cheeses warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; the use of organic extra virgin olive oil as the primary dressing fat provides oleic acid and polyphenols that support endothelial function, but portion size should be limited to half a pizza (200 g) to keep saturated fat intake within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Raw red beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the raw Argentine beef base, which is a primary dietary RA trigger; cherry tomatoes (nightshade) may also exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals, and substituting beef with smoked salmon carpaccio would substantially reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carpaccio di Manzo Pizza is an Italian-inspired fusion dish served in Singapore, consisting of a 12-inch baked pizza base topped with thinly sliced raw Argentine beef carpaccio, fresh cherry tomatoes, shaved Parmesan cheese, peppery rocket leaves (arugula), a drizzle of 25-year aged balsamic dressing, organic extra virgin olive oil, and mozzarella cheese. Unlike conventional pizzas, the carpaccio toppings are assembled cold after the base is baked, preserving the delicate texture and flavour of the raw beef. The result is a dish with a crisp, light base contrasted by silky, umami-rich beef, sharp Parmesan, tangy balsamic, and fresh greens. Nutritionally, this pizza is atypical: it is very high in protein (approximately 16.7 g/100g) and moderate in fat (12 g/100g), but extremely low in carbohydrates (3.33 g/100g) relative to standard pizza, owing to the dominance of raw beef and cheese over dough. The glycaemic load per full serving is low (~6), making it one of the more diabetes-friendly pizza variants. Sodium is moderate at 300 mg/100g, primarily from Parmesan and mozzarella. The dish contains saturated fat and trans fat from beef and aged cheese, which warrants moderation for cardiovascular patients. Extra virgin olive oil provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fats and polyphenols. The dish is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the high arachidonic acid content of raw red meat. It is not halal-certified and contains gluten (pizza base) and dairy allergens. Known in Chinese as 牛肉薄片披萨, in Malay as Pizza Karpacio Daging Lembu, and available at Italian restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is carpaccio di manzo pizza healthy?
How many calories in beef carpaccio pizza?
Is carpaccio pizza good for diabetics?
Is beef carpaccio pizza high in protein?
Can I eat carpaccio pizza if I have high blood pressure?
What toppings are on carpaccio di manzo pizza?
Is raw beef pizza safe to eat?

confidence_reason: Input values are AI-generated (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) from a GrabFood listing. No exact match exists in any approved primary source for this composite dish. Nutrient values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central component entries for raw beef, cheese pizza, arugula, cherry tomatoes, Parmesan, mozzarella, balsamic vinegar, and olive oil, and are broadly consistent. Medium confidence is assigned because the values are plausible but not directly verified from a single approved source measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central component references as no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Input AI-generated values cross-checked and retained as broadly consistent. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid disaggregation for this composite beef carpaccio pizza is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific composite dish not available in approved sources as of 2024. 3. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as gluten_free in dietary_tags; the pizza base contains gluten and this flag has been corrected to false. 4. The input ethnic_tags included 'Chicken Dish' and 'Pasta' which are incorrect for this dish; corrected to Italian and Pizza only. 5. Two portions were listed in the input with is_default: true for both; the 400g whole pizza has been selected as the primary serving size for GL calculation. 6. GI estimated from thin-crust cheese pizza analogue (GI ~45) per Atkinson et al. 2008; this is a conservative upper-bound given the very low carbohydrate content of this dish. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.33 g/100g as reported in the input, consistent with naturally occurring trans fats (conjugated linoleic acid) in beef and dairy; this is not an industrially added trans fat. 8. The dish is not halal-certified as it is served at a restaurant without confirmed halal certification and contains raw beef of unspecified slaughter method. 9. Multilingual local names added for Singaporean compliance: Chinese (牛肉薄片披萨 / 牛肉薄片披薩), Malay (Pizza Karpacio Daging Lembu), Tamil (மாட்டிறைச்சி கார்பாச்சியோ பிட்சா), Hindi (बीफ कार्पाचियो पिज़्ज़ा). These are descriptive translations applied because the dish is listed and served in Singapore. 10. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value) from 'broader' used in iteration 2.

Iteration 3: Added all five new multilingual strings to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy Q8 consistency requirement.
